title: Fitting a wall-mounted wooden handrail
subtitle: Fix a continuous timber handrail to a staircase wall on bracket fixings
excerpt: How to mark out, fix wall brackets at the correct height and pitch, cut the handrail to length, and secure it to the brackets so that the rail runs continuously at a consistent height and at the same angle as the stair flight.
sourceNotes: Building Regulations Part K (2013). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Timber Trade Federation handrail installation guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The angle at which a handrail rises along a stair flight, matched to the pitch of the staircase. A wall-mounted handrail must be fixed at a consistent height above the nosing line (Building Regulations Part K requires this to be 900–1000 mm for domestic stairs) and at the same angle as the stair.

A metal or hardwood bracket fixed to the wall that supports the underside of the handrail at intervals of 900 to 1200 mm. The bracket must be fixed into a stud or masonry — a plasterboard-only fixing cannot take the dynamic lateral load of someone gripping a rail.

A nylon or fibre insert pushed into a masonry drill hole that grips the thread of a screw as it is driven in, providing a secure fixing in brick, block, or stone where a screw alone would pull out. Match the plug colour to the screw diameter: red plugs take No. 8 screws, brown plugs take No. 10 screws.

content.text: A wall-mounted handrail must be fixed at a constant height above the stair nosing line: not above the floor at each tread, and not level. The

of the rail must match the angle of the stair. The critical measurement to establish before marking anything on the wall is the vertical height from each tread nosing to the underside of the handrail: Building Regulations Part K (England and Wales) requires this to be between 900 mm and 1000 mm on domestic stairs.

Hold a straightedge against three or four nosings to establish the nosing line: the imaginary line connecting the front edges of all the treads. Mark the height of 950 mm (the midpoint of the permitted range) directly above each nosing mark on the wall and snap a chalk line between these points. This chalk line shows where the top of the handrail will be; subtract the rail depth to find the bracket top position.
Step 2, locate stud or masonry and mark bracket positions

On a stud wall, use an electronic stud finder and mark the stud centres. Space brackets at 900 to 1200 mm intervals, adjusting positions to hit studs wherever possible. On a masonry wall, brackets can be fixed anywhere along the line using the SDS drill to bore masonry fixings holes and
. Mark the bracket positions on the chalk line. Fix the top and bottom brackets first, then string a line between them to position the intermediate brackets accurately.
Drill fixing holes at each bracket position. For masonry, use the SDS drill with a masonry bit, insert

, and drive No. 10 × 50 mm screws. For stud walls, drive No. 10 × 70 mm screws directly into the stud through the plasterboard. Check each bracket with the spirit level: they must all be at the same angle to the slope of the stair, not level. A small angle gauge or bevel set to the stair pitch helps here.
Step 4, cut and fix the handrail

Measure the full run of the handrail from the bottom newel cap to the top landing. Mark and cut square or at 45° as required. Lay the handrail into the brackets and check it sits at the correct height at each bracket position. Fix the rail to each
with the supplied screws driven up through the bracket into the underside of the rail. Sand the cut ends smooth with the sanding block and apply the chosen finish to the bare cut face.

Some handrail profiles have a flat groove milled into the underside. This locates onto a ridge on the bracket and prevents lateral movement without additional screws. Where the handrail ends against a wall instead of meeting a newel, fit a turned wall return or scroll end to cap the exposed grain and remove the sharp edge.

attrs.symptom: Handrail height varies from one end of the stair to the other
attrs.cause: Brackets not set at a consistent height above the nosing line; chalk line not established or ignored.
attrs.fix: Remove the rail, re-establish the nosing line and chalk line, reposition the brackets, and refit the rail. Height consistency is more important than level.

attrs.symptom: Bracket fixing pulls loose from the wall
attrs.cause: Fixed only into plasterboard rather than a stud; masonry plug too small for the screw.
attrs.fix: Locate the stud and refix with longer screws driven into solid timber. In masonry, redrill with a larger bit and use a heavier plug.

attrs.symptom: Handrail wobbles laterally even though brackets are secure
attrs.cause: Rail not screwed down into the brackets; rail slot not engaging the bracket ridge.
attrs.fix: Drive screws up through the bracket into the underside of the rail at each bracket position. Do not rely on friction alone.
